title: Carrot cold-process soap
subtitle: A golden-orange bar made with carrot juice as the water substitute, olive oil, and coconut oil
excerpt: Replacing the lye solution water with fresh carrot juice produces a bar with a warm golden-orange colour from the beta-carotene in the carrots. The juice sugars also accelerate trace slightly, so this is a recipe to work quickly. Oil base is olive, coconut, and castor oil at 5% superfat.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied. Carrot juice as lye solution liquid: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), natural liquid substitutes chapter. Beta-carotene in soap colouring: Bramble Berry colourant reference (2023). Note: carrot juice must be freshly pressed and strained; bottled juice with preservatives can inhibit cure.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate the lye weight using a SAP calculator before scaling. Do not multiply the lye weight directly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Carrot juice can be pressed and refrigerated the day before. Bring to room temperature before using as the lye liquid.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark cupboard. Discard if bars develop orange oily flecks (DOS) or a rancid smell. The carrot-derived colour fades to a pale peach over the cure period; this is normal.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the lye and oil mixture emulsifies to a custard-like consistency. Drizzled soap holds its shape briefly on the surface.
The percentage of unsaponified oil remaining in a finished bar, added for a mild, conditioning result. A 5% superfat is typical for everyday bars.

content.text: This recipe uses freshly pressed carrot juice in place of distilled water when making the lye solution. The beta-carotene in the juice gives the finished bar a warm golden-orange tone. The sugars in the juice accelerate
, so pour the batter into the mould promptly once colour is added. The bar hardens over 4 to 6 weeks, during which the colour shifts from bright orange to a warm peach.

Press the carrots and strain the juice to remove pulp. Refrigerate until very cold or freeze in an ice-cube tray. Using chilled or partly frozen juice slows the heat spike when the lye is added, reducing the risk of scorching the sugars (which can give the finished bar a brown tinge rather than orange).
Work in a ventilated space. Wear safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves before touching lye. Weigh all ingredients and have the mould and tools ready before mixing the lye solution.

Weigh the cold carrot juice into the HDPE pitcher. Add the sodium hydroxide slowly in a thin stream, stirring continuously. The sugar content may cause the solution to darken slightly; this is normal. The solution still heats to around 70 to 80 degrees C. Do not lean over the pitcher. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C before combining with the oils.
Melt the coconut oil if solid and combine with the olive oil and castor oil. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C.

Pour the lye solution into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend in short pulses to light trace. Work quickly once trace is reached, as the sugars in the carrot juice accelerate the emulsion. Pour into the mould and smooth the surface. Cover and wrap in a towel to hold the heat.

Unmould after 48 hours. The bars may be darker than expected at this stage; the colour lightens as the bar hardens. Cut into bars 2.5 cm thick and place on a rack. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dry place. At 4 weeks the
oils have had time to distribute evenly through the bar and the pH has dropped to a mild level.

Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark cupboard on an open soap dish. The warm colour fades gently over the cure period and with exposure to light; this does not affect the bar's performance. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots (DOS) or an off smell.
